items.html: Define mental health and emotional functioning
Identify indicators of positive mental health and wellbeing
Recognize mental health challenges faced by children, adolescents, and adults with SCD
Identify the impact of mental health challenges on those with SCD and effective strategies for prevention and management

questions.question: My knowledge about Mental Health and Wellness in Sickle Cell Disease on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My comfort level with Mental Health and Wellness in SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My confidence level with Mental Health and Wellness for people with SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .

data.html: Positive mental health or emotional functioning is an essential contributor to the health and well-being of individuals with SCD. With the benefit of research and observations from clinical settings, we understand better than ever the close relationship between mind, mood, cognitive functioning and physical health.

Good mental health and well-being in individuals with SCD may be associated with: Happiness or life satisfaction levels • Spirituality • Positive outlook • Hope • Thinking and problem-solving • Coping • Adjustment • Degree of social support • Sense of agency or control over life circumstances • Freedom from unnecessary suffering

Patients may experience or report the above as: Having a clear mind and the ability to process information and set goals • Ability to pursue meaningful work and to function on the job • Having language to communicate wants and needs with ease • Experiencing restorative sleep • Access to timely and effective healthcare
These are factors to consider when supporting good mental health and well-being for people with SCD.

slides.title: Positive Mental Health & Wellbeing in SCD

items.html: There is a growing body of research focusing on mental health in individuals with SCD across the lifespan. So let's look at some of the general trends by age group, starting with Children and Adolescents.
Increased risk of mood, anxiety, attention, and behaviour regulation challenges compared to peers without SCD.
Stress and depression may be worsened by pain severity and frequency of pain episodes.
High risk of overt and 'silent' stroke in the early childhood period may have linkages to behavioural, learning and social emotional sequelae for many youth.
Isolation and disruption in peer relationships is a contributor to mood, anxiety, and social skill difficulties.

Development of independent and self-help skills or 'developmental tasks of adolescence' may be slowed or delayed by interruption of home, school, family, and social routines due to a challenging illness course, including the unpredictable nature of pain episodes.

Body image disturbance and self-esteem challenges may be exacerbated by differences in physical maturation, physical appearance, endurance and stamina levels, and attainment of related milestones compared to peers without SCD.

slides.title: Mental Health Challenges in Adults with SCD

items.title: Mood and Anxiety Difficulties
items.html: Adults with SCD are at significant risk for mental health difficulties, especially mood (depression) and anxiety disorders.

items.title: Prevalence
items.html: Prevalence rates of greater than 40% for depression and approximately 25% for anxiety have been reported in research samples of adult sickle cell patients.

items.title: Disability
items.html: Interference of SCD symptoms on daily functioning increases risk of low mood (depression), anxiety, and social isolation due to difficulties engaging with school and work.

items.title: Stroke
items.html: Stroke and risk of stroke recurrence continues to be of concern during the adult years and is correlated with decreased physical, cognitive and emotional functioning.

items.title: Chronic Pain
items.html: Chronic pain may co-occur with acute pain episodes at this life stage, and severity of disease-related complications tends to be greater with increasing age.

items.title: Pain Management Challenges
items.html: Adults with SCD continue to report significant undertreatment of pain in emergency or inpatient settings. Importantly, problematic addiction, substance misuse or 'drug seeking' patterns have not been substantiated in patients with SCD, who benefit from well-developed acute and chronic pain management support to reduce suffering.

images.caption: Disease knowledge and expertise about the treatments that work best for them
Spirituality or faith traditions that foster hope, a sense of meaning and enduring purpose beyond their health status
Linkage to advocacy and support groups

data.bubble_text:
Providing access to effective, culturally-appropriate psychotherapeutic interventions that teach positive coping skills, including:
  • Use of proactive pain coping strategies
  • Use of mindfulness and cognitive strategies (realistic thinking; thought restructuring)
  • Employing emotion regulation strategies
  • Support for adaptive activity and physical functioning
  • Engagement in relaxation practice
  • Connection to social support
  • Problem-solving support

The RBCD Hub's article, Sickle Cell Disease and Mental Health, provides an overview of the links between physical and mental health: https://www.rbcd.ca/blog/sickle-cell-disease-and-mental-health

How can sickle cell disease affect mental health? https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/mental-health-and-sickle-cell-disease

slides.title: When Do You Reach Out For Mental Health Support?

data.title: When Do You Reach Out For Mental Health Support?

items.html: Mental health is something that everyone possesses
As individuals living with chronic illness, youth and adults with sickle cell disease are at increased risk for mental health concerns, especially mood and anxiety challenges

Living with complications of sickle cell disease can negatively affect mental health and wellbeing, as can encounters with healthcare practitioners and systems that are not well-informed about this condition, the need for timely treatment, and maintains barriers to support and needed resources

There are a number of indicators of positive mental health and well-being that individuals with SCD and their caregivers may identify, including hope, connection to spirituality and social support and positive coping

Conducting timely mental health screening and providing appropriate mental and physical treatment interventions can significantly improve mental health and well-being outcomes for individuals with SCD
